author: adam
title: Understanding the CAP Theorem
summary: A practical look at consistency, availability, and partition tolerance — and why you can only pick two during a network partition.

author: jane
title: Eventual Consistency in Practice
summary: How distributed systems converge to a consistent state over time, and the patterns that make it workable.

author: adam
title: Domain-Driven Design: Strategic Patterns
summary: Bounded contexts, ubiquitous language, and context maps — the high-level tools for taming large domains.

author: jane
title: Domain-Driven Design: Aggregates & Invariants
summary: What aggregates are, how to draw their boundaries, and why external code should only talk to the root.

author: adam
title: Value Objects in Domain-Driven Design
summary: Objects defined by their attributes rather than identity — and why immutability makes them safe to share.

author: jane
title: CQRS: Command Query Responsibility Segregation
summary: Separating the write model from the read model to let each evolve independently and scale differently.

author: adam
title: Event Sourcing: Storing State as a Sequence of Events
summary: Instead of persisting current state, store every change as an immutable event and derive state by replaying the log.

author: jane
title: Microservices Architecture: Promises & Pitfalls
summary: Independent deployability and polyglot freedom come with distributed systems complexity — here is what to expect.

author: adam
title: Hexagonal Architecture: Ports and Adapters
summary: Isolate your domain from infrastructure by inverting dependencies — the application core knows nothing about HTTP, databases, or queues.

author: jane
title: Clean Architecture: The Dependency Rule
summary: Source code dependencies must always point inward — toward higher-level policy and away from details like frameworks and databases.

author: adam
title: Monolith vs Microservices: When to Split
summary: A decision framework for choosing between a modular monolith and microservices — and the signals that tell you it is time to split.

author: jane
title: The API Gateway Pattern
summary: A single entry point for clients that handles routing, auth, rate limiting, and protocol translation across backend services.

author: adam
title: The Saga Pattern: Distributed Transactions Without 2PC
summary: Coordinate long-running business transactions across services using a sequence of local transactions and compensating actions.

author: jane
title: The Circuit Breaker Pattern
summary: Stop cascading failures by detecting when a downstream service is unhealthy and short-circuiting calls until it recovers.

author: adam
title: The Outbox Pattern for Reliable Messaging
summary: Guarantee at-least-once event delivery without two-phase commit by writing events to the database in the same transaction as your business data.

author: jane
title: The Repository Pattern
summary: Mediate between the domain model and the data mapping layer using a collection-like interface for accessing domain objects.

author: adam
title: The Factory Pattern: Centralising Object Creation
summary: Delegate the responsibility of creating complex objects to a dedicated factory, keeping construction logic out of the domain and callers.

author: jane
title: The Strategy Pattern: Encapsulating Algorithms
summary: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable — open for extension, closed for modification.

author: adam
title: The Observer Pattern and Event-Driven Design
summary: Decouple publishers from subscribers by letting objects register interest in events without the producer knowing who is listening.

author: jane
title: Dependency Injection and IoC Containers
summary: Invert control of dependency creation to keep classes decoupled from their collaborators and trivially testable.
